refined_fr.refined_fr: CR-NGHE-003 Refined: The NGHE solution shall enable operators and tele-operators to achieve a measured energy efficiency of energy_per_ton ≤ E_target (customer-defined threshold) over the agreed evaluation period, through integrated monitoring, control optimization, and real-time feedback mechanisms.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR is outcome-focused but lacks explicit connection to the mechanisms (monitoring, control, feedback) that enable efficiency achievement. Refinement clarifies that energy efficiency is not a passive property but an active capability requiring measurement, optimization logic, and operator awareness. This supports both direct operation and teleoperation modes.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Measure energy_per_ton = total_energy_consumed / total_tonnage_moved over a defined evaluation period (e.g., 8-hour shift, multi-day project phase). Verify that measured value ≤ E_target. Collect telemetry from Vehicle power systems and cross-reference with load/distance data. Conduct acceptance testing under representative worksite conditions with Site Manager and Equipment Operator oversight.
sub_frs: CR-NGHE-003.1: The NGHE solution shall continuously measure and log energy consumption (kWh or equivalent) from all propulsion and auxiliary systems.
CR-NGHE-003.2: The NGHE solution shall continuously measure and log work output (tonnage moved, distance traveled, or equivalent productivity metric).
CR-NGHE-003.3: The NGHE solution shall compute and display real-time energy_per_ton ratio to the operator and tele-operator.
CR-NGHE-003.4: The NGHE solution shall provide control recommendations or automated optimization to reduce energy_per_ton below E_target.
CR-NGHE-003.5: The NGHE solution shall log and report energy efficiency metrics to the Site Manager and Maintenance team for post-shift analysis and continuous improvement.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement a centralized energy management system (EMS) that aggregates power telemetry from Vehicle subsystems, computes energy_per_ton in real-time, and broadcasts efficiency metrics via Telemetry interface to operator displays and backend analytics.
DP-B: Distribute energy monitoring across Vehicle subsystems with local optimization logic; aggregate results at the NGHE Solution level for reporting only, without real-time feedback to operators.

DP-C: Implement predictive energy modeling in the Teleoperation module to forecast energy_per_ton based on planned tasks and provide pre-task optimization recommendations to tele-operators before task execution.

DP-D: Implement a hybrid approach combining real-time centralized measurement (DP-A) with predictive modeling (DP-C) and local Vehicle-level optimization, coordinated via Tasking and Telemetry interfaces.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-D: Hybrid Energy Efficiency Architecture – Implement a three-tier energy management system: (1) Real-time measurement and aggregation at NGHE Solution level via Telemetry interface from Vehicle power subsystems; (2) Predictive energy modeling in Teleoperation module to forecast energy_per_ton for planned tasks and recommend optimizations before execution; (3) Local Vehicle-level optimization logic that adjusts propulsion and auxiliary systems based on real-time efficiency feedback. Coordinate all three tiers via Tasking (task parameters to Teleoperation and Vehicle) and Telemetry (efficiency metrics back to operators and Site Manager). This approach balances real-time responsiveness (DP-A), predictive planning (DP-C), and distributed autonomy (DP-B).

potential_sources: Coupling between Vehicle-level optimization and Teleoperation predictive model: both may recommend conflicting control actions. Mitigation: establish priority rules (e.g., Teleoperation recommendations override local optimization if tele-operator is actively controlling) and define handoff protocols.

Coupling between real-time measurement (NGHE Solution) and Teleoperation forecasting: forecasts must be validated against actual measured efficiency. Mitigation: implement feedback loop to update predictive model parameters based on measured outcomes.

Coupling between Tasking interface (task parameters) and Vehicle optimization: task complexity may exceed Vehicle's local optimization capability. Mitigation: define task complexity thresholds; escalate complex tasks to Teleoperation for human-guided optimization.
coupling_check.notes: DP-D introduces moderate coupling across three subsystems but is justified by the need to balance operator autonomy, tele-operator oversight, and automated optimization. Coupling is manageable via well-defined interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry) and priority rules.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Vehicle power subsystems can provide real-time energy consumption telemetry with sufficient granularity (per-subsystem or per-function) and accuracy (±5% or better). Risk: Legacy or third-party Vehicle components may not expose this data. Mitigation: conduct early Vehicle interface audit; design adapter layers if needed.

Assumption: Teleoperation module has sufficient computational capacity to run predictive energy models in near-real-time (sub-second latency). Risk: Network latency or Teleoperation server overload may delay recommendations. Mitigation: implement local caching of model parameters; allow Vehicle to proceed with local optimization if Teleoperation recommendations are delayed.

Assumption: Operators and tele-operators will actively use energy efficiency feedback to modify behavior. Risk: Behavioral adoption may be low if feedback is not salient or actionable. Mitigation: design operator UI to highlight efficiency gains/losses; provide gamification or incentive mechanisms; conduct operator training.

Assumption: E_target is achievable with the selected Vehicle and worksite conditions. Risk: E_target may be unrealistic, leading to chronic failure. Mitigation: conduct feasibility study during requirements phase; baseline energy_per_ton under current operations; set E_target with 10-15% improvement margin.

Assumption: Safety criticality (high) does not conflict with energy optimization. Risk: Aggressive optimization may compromise safety (e.g., reduced braking energy, faster cycle times). Mitigation: define safety constraints in optimization logic; Safety Services module must validate all optimization recommendations.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-D to explicitly include a Safety Services integration point: 'DP-D shall include a safety validation gate in the Teleoperation predictive model and Vehicle optimization logic. Any energy optimization recommendation that would reduce safety margins (e.g., braking distance, stability) shall be flagged to Safety Services and require explicit operator override or shall be rejected.' This ensures that energy efficiency does not degrade safety.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: The NGHE Solution module owns the end-to-end energy efficiency capability, including: (1) real-time aggregation of energy telemetry from Vehicle subsystems via Telemetry interface; (2) computation of energy_per_ton KPI; (3) coordination of Teleoperation predictive recommendations and Vehicle local optimization via Tasking and Telemetry interfaces; (4) logging and reporting of efficiency metrics to Site Manager and Maintenance team; (5) integration with Safety Services for safety-constrained optimization.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Inbound from Vehicle: real-time power consumption (kW), energy accumulator (kWh), propulsion subsystem status, auxiliary subsystem status, load/distance metrics. Frequency: 1 Hz or higher. Used for real-time energy_per_ton computation and operator feedback.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Outbound to Teleoperation and operator displays: computed energy_per_ton ratio, efficiency trend, optimization recommendations, alerts. Frequency: 1 Hz or higher. Used for operator awareness and tele-operator decision support.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Outbound to Vehicle and Teleoperation: task parameters (load, distance, priority, safety constraints). Used to communicate task context for predictive modeling and local optimization.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Inbound from Site Manager or Teleoperation: task requests with energy efficiency targets or constraints. Used to set optimization objectives.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional with Safety Services: NGHE Solution sends optimization recommendations for safety validation; Safety Services returns approval/rejection and safety constraints. Used to ensure energy optimization does not compromise safety.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the natural owner of the end-to-end energy efficiency KPI and coordination logic. It aggregates telemetry from Vehicle, coordinates with Teleoperation and Safety Services, and reports to Site Manager. Minimal diffusion: only Vehicle-level optimization logic (sub-component of Vehicle module) is distributed.

Vehicle could own local energy measurement and optimization, but lacks visibility into Teleoperation predictive model and Site Manager reporting requirements. Containment score reflects that Vehicle owns propulsion/auxiliary subsystems and local optimization, but energy_per_ton KPI computation and cross-system coordination must reside at NGHE Solution level. Diffusion risk: if Vehicle owns measurement, NGHE Solution must duplicate aggregation logic.

Teleoperation could own predictive energy modeling and tele-operator-facing recommendations, but lacks direct access to Vehicle telemetry and Site Manager reporting. Containment score reflects that Teleoperation is a consumer of energy efficiency data, not the owner of the KPI. Diffusion risk: if Teleoperation owns measurement, it duplicates NGHE Solution logic.

Safety Services is a validator/constraint provider, not an owner of energy efficiency. Containment score reflects that Safety Services provides safety constraints to energy optimization, but does not own the optimization logic itself. Diffusion risk: high if Safety Services tries to own optimization; instead, it should provide constraints via SafetyAlert interface.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: NGHE Solution is the optimal owner because: (1) it has system-level visibility into Vehicle telemetry, Teleoperation recommendations, and Site Manager requirements; (2) it can compute and report the energy_per_ton KPI without duplication; (3) it can coordinate optimization across Vehicle and Teleoperation modules via well-defined interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert); (4) it can enforce safety constraints via Safety Services integration. Vehicle and Teleoperation are consumers/contributors, not owners.
containment_risks: Risk: NGHE Solution becomes a 'god module' if it owns too much logic. Mitigation: clearly separate concerns – NGHE Solution owns KPI computation and coordination; Vehicle owns local optimization; Teleoperation owns predictive modeling. Use interfaces to enforce boundaries.

Risk: Telemetry interface may become a bottleneck if Vehicle sends too much data. Mitigation: define a minimal telemetry schema (power, energy, load, distance); compress or aggregate data at Vehicle level before transmission.

Risk: Safety Services integration via SafetyAlert may introduce latency if validation is synchronous. Mitigation: implement asynchronous validation with fallback to conservative (safe) optimization if Safety Services is unavailable.

Risk: Site Manager reporting may require historical data that NGHE Solution must store and query. Mitigation: design NGHE Solution with persistent logging capability; consider offloading to a separate analytics module if data volume is large.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Explicitly define the IM boundary to include: (1) Real-time energy aggregation and KPI computation engine; (2) Coordination logic for Vehicle and Teleoperation optimization; (3) Safety constraint enforcement via SafetyAlert interface; (4) Logging and reporting subsystem for Site Manager and Maintenance. Exclude: Vehicle propulsion/auxiliary control (owned by Vehicle), Teleoperation predictive modeling (owned by Teleoperation), Safety validation logic (owned by Safety Services). This clarifies ownership and reduces diffusion risk.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-D (Hybrid Energy Efficiency Architecture) directly addresses the refined FR by providing: (1) real-time measurement of energy consumption and work output (FR sub-requirements 003.1, 003.2); (2) real-time computation and display of energy_per_ton (FR 003.3); (3) optimization recommendations and local control (FR 003.4); (4) logging and reporting (FR 003.5). DP-D is not over-constrained and allows flexibility in implementation details (e.g., optimization algorithm, feedback frequency). Coupling is manageable via well-defined interfaces.

diffusion_risks: Diffusion Risk 1: Energy measurement logic may be duplicated if both NGHE Solution and Vehicle implement telemetry aggregation. Mitigation: clearly assign measurement responsibility to Vehicle (raw data collection) and NGHE Solution (aggregation and KPI computation). Define Telemetry interface schema to prevent duplication.

Diffusion Risk 2: Optimization logic may be scattered across Vehicle (local), Teleoperation (predictive), and NGHE Solution (coordination). Mitigation: define clear ownership – Vehicle owns local reactive optimization; Teleoperation owns predictive planning; NGHE Solution owns coordination and safety validation. Use Tasking interface to communicate optimization directives.

Diffusion Risk 3: Safety constraint enforcement may be duplicated if both NGHE Solution and Safety Services implement validation. Mitigation: NGHE Solution requests validation via SafetyAlert; Safety Services provides constraints; NGHE Solution applies constraints. No duplication of validation logic.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution is the optimal container for DP-D because it has system-level visibility and can coordinate across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services without duplication. Vehicle and Teleoperation are contributors (via Telemetry and Tasking interfaces), not owners. Containment score of 95 for NGHE Solution reflects strong alignment.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification of CR-NGHE-003 is feasible via: (1) Instrumentation of Vehicle power subsystems to measure energy consumption; (2) Instrumentation of load/distance sensors to measure work output; (3) Real-time computation of energy_per_ton in NGHE Solution; (4) Acceptance testing over a defined evaluation period (e.g., 8-hour shift) with Site Manager and Equipment Operator oversight. Verification can be automated via telemetry logging and post-shift analysis. High confidence in feasibility.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation of CR-NGHE-003 to NGHE Solution is clear. Sub-requirements are allocated as follows: 003.1 (measurement) -> Vehicle (data collection) + NGHE Solution (aggregation); 003.2 (work output) -> Vehicle (sensors) + NGHE Solution (aggregation); 003.3 (display) -> NGHE Solution (computation) + Teleoperation (operator UI); 003.4 (optimization) -> Vehicle (local) + Teleoperation (predictive) + NGHE Solution (coordination); 003.5 (reporting) -> NGHE Solution (logging) + Site Manager (analysis). Allocation is clear and traceable.

recommended_revisions: Revision 1: Add explicit safety validation gate to DP-D. Recommendation: 'All energy optimization recommendations shall be validated by Safety Services via SafetyAlert interface before execution. Recommendations that would reduce safety margins shall be rejected or require explicit operator override.'

Revision 2: Define Telemetry interface schema in detail. Recommendation: Specify minimum telemetry fields (power, energy, load, distance, subsystem status), data types, units, frequency, and compression/aggregation rules to prevent diffusion and ensure interoperability.

Revision 3: Define Tasking interface for optimization directives. Recommendation: Specify task parameters (load, distance, priority, safety constraints, energy target), communication protocol, and priority rules (e.g., Teleoperation recommendations override local optimization if tele-operator is actively controlling).

Revision 4: Conduct feasibility study on E_target. Recommendation: Baseline energy_per_ton under current operations; set E_target with 10-15% improvement margin; validate that DP-D can achieve E_target without compromising safety or operator workload.

decision.justification: CR-NGHE-003 is not atomic and requires further decomposition. The refined FR identifies five sub-requirements (003.1 through 003.5) that must be individually designed and allocated to modules. DP-D provides a coherent architecture, but each sub-requirement needs its own FR-DP-IM decomposition to ensure completeness and traceability. NGHE Solution is the correct owner, but the next level must detail: (1) energy measurement and aggregation logic; (2) energy_per_ton computation algorithm; (3) real-time feedback mechanism; (4) optimization recommendation logic; (5) logging and reporting subsystem. Proceeding to the next decomposition level is necessary to achieve sufficient design detail for implementation.
decision.justification_detail: The gate review identified no blocking issues (all gates passed), but the complexity of DP-D and the presence of five sub-requirements indicate that further decomposition is required. The refined FR is well-structured and the selected DP is sound, but the IM assignment (NGHE Solution) is at a high level and must be decomposed into sub-modules or design components. Proceeding with ZIGZAG_DOWN allows the design to mature while maintaining traceability and reducing risk.
next_level_sub_frs: CR-NGHE-003.1: The NGHE solution shall continuously measure and log energy consumption (kWh or equivalent) from all propulsion and auxiliary systems.
CR-NGHE-003.2: The NGHE solution shall continuously measure and log work output (tonnage moved, distance traveled, or equivalent productivity metric).
CR-NGHE-003.3: The NGHE solution shall compute and display real-time energy_per_ton ratio to the operator and tele-operator.
CR-NGHE-003.4: The NGHE solution shall provide control recommendations or automated optimization to reduce energy_per_ton below E_target.
CR-NGHE-003.5: The NGHE solution shall log and report energy efficiency metrics to the Site Manager and Maintenance team for post-shift analysis and continuous improvement.

assumptions: Vehicle power subsystems can provide real-time energy consumption telemetry with ±5% accuracy or better.
Teleoperation module has sufficient computational capacity to run predictive energy models with sub-second latency.
Operators and tele-operators will actively use energy efficiency feedback to modify behavior.
E_target is achievable with the selected Vehicle and worksite conditions (10-15% improvement margin assumed).
Safety criticality (high) can be managed via Safety Services integration without blocking energy optimization.
Telemetry, Tasking, and SafetyAlert interfaces are available and can support the required data rates and latencies.
open_risks: Risk: Vehicle power subsystems may not expose real-time energy consumption data. Mitigation: conduct early Vehicle interface audit; design adapter layers if needed.
Risk: Teleoperation server may become a bottleneck if predictive modeling is computationally expensive. Mitigation: implement local caching and fallback to Vehicle local optimization.
Risk: Behavioral adoption of energy efficiency feedback may be low. Mitigation: design salient operator UI; provide gamification or incentive mechanisms; conduct operator training.
Risk: E_target may be unrealistic. Mitigation: conduct feasibility study; baseline current energy_per_ton; set E_target with improvement margin.
Risk: Safety Services validation may introduce latency. Mitigation: implement asynchronous validation with conservative fallback.
Risk: Diffusion of optimization logic across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and NGHE Solution. Mitigation: clearly define ownership and use interfaces to enforce boundaries.
evidence_needed: Vehicle interface specification: power subsystem telemetry schema, data types, units, frequency, accuracy.
Teleoperation computational capacity assessment: predictive model complexity, latency requirements, server specifications.
Operator behavior study: baseline energy efficiency awareness, adoption of feedback mechanisms, training effectiveness.
Feasibility study: baseline energy_per_ton under current operations, E_target setting, improvement margin validation.
Safety Services integration specification: SafetyAlert interface schema, validation latency, constraint types.
Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert interface specifications: data schemas, communication protocols, priority rules.
Acceptance test plan: evaluation period definition, measurement methodology, success criteria (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target).
